speaker: interviewee
text: When we started to be involved in the middle of the war, I remember the first airplanes, they came over, Vyshnivets, and dropped only two bombs, on the bridge between the crossing the [NON-ENGLISH]. But I'll never forget the sound of falling bombs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Whose airplanes were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: German planes. And they weren't big bombs. But the sound of a falling bomb is an experience which one never forgets. It's a shrill. And it's the fact that not knowing where the bomb is going to hit. Is it going to fall on top of our house? On top of our neighbor's house? And I remember when the impact finally came, the doors sprung open; a couple of windows broke. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] So we knew then that war was on. And of course, as history has recorded, it wasn't very long before we started seeing the first Russian soldiers in retreat. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I remember in particular when the group of the men that were taken to the army from Vyshnivets. They had their own group. And when that group passed through town on retreat, because we recognized everybody. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And--

speaker: interviewer
text: You mean these were the Jewish people who went into the Soviet Army?

speaker: interviewee
text: People that the Army took and conscripted. I don't know whether the right word-- mobilized. They mobilized. And, you know, many people had a brother, a sister, a son that started following the Russian politruc, which was in the political commissar, which was in charge, drew his gun, and shook his head, and he waved everybody away. People followed. People cried. But they soon enough passed, and they were gone. [COUGHS] The uncertainty of transition from the Russian occupation to the reoccupation of the Romanians and Germans left a void for three or four days. It was very quiet. We didn't know who was in charge. More and more Russian soldiers in smaller groups were retreating. At about the third or fourth day, about 10 days into the war, I believe, all of a sudden, it was very quiet. Dead quiet. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In order to kind of be together with other Jewish people while this was happening, we-- my dad, and my mother, and my brother, and myself, my younger brother Joe-- went over to our neighbor across the street. And other families. We had a big house. He had a big house. And we felt safer together. And so we were there. We stayed there overnight. We locked the doors in our house. Stayed there overnight. Nobody slept. The next day around noon, all of a sudden, the quiet came to an end. And we heard motorcycles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Because we were afraid to look out of the windows.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I remember peeping out through a hole, through a keyhole. And what I saw, I'll never forget. Motorcycles. This was in July. This was in-- in early July. Beautiful, sunny day. Sky was blue. Motorcycles. Soldiers with their machine guns in the front. Clean, organized. It was almost beautiful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they were rushing through town. And then another group of motorcycles. And then some motorized soldiers, some vehicles. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Not a shot. Not a shot. Until later that night and early the next morning, we started hearing sporadic shots. We didn't know what those shots were. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] That-- towards evening of the second day, some people that my dad knew, some Ukrainian people, came to warn us that there's a good chance that we might get killed. That a lot of Jewish people have already been killed. Simply shot. What the Romanians did, they declared three-day free-- open season on the Jewish population of Vyshnivets. When the three days were over, they decreed that the open season was finished, that we could come out of our houses. Of course, you know, we didn't know whether we were going to be alive at the end of three days or not. But when it was all over, we found out that several hundred Jewish men and women, including my grandfather, my mother's father [NON-ENGLISH], was murdered, killed, shot in cold blood. My grandfather was killed by a young Ukrainian fascist.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they killed in their own home?

speaker: interviewee
text: My grandfather was killed in front of his house. He had a house in the country. Both my grandfathers and my grandparents had parcels of land, several hectares of land. They were peaceful people. Worked on their land. Tried to provide for their families. He was killed on his own front yard.

speaker: interviewer
text: The other people, we had a-- a neighbor. They took him and his son. He was-- he used to [NON-ENGLISH]. Headstones? He used to-- Monuments?

speaker: interviewee
text: Monuments. Yeah. [SNEEZES] They took him, and his son out, and they shot them both. Another neighbor of ours, I believe his name was [? Ozzie ?] [? Hoffman. ?] He was a tailor. They took him out and shot him in front of his wife. Anyway, this is what happened. On the third day, the fourth day, I remember people started organizing the mass burial of all the people that died in the Programme. By that time, of course, the Romanian authorities had re-established their position. And a new-- a new era of living started for the people of Vyshnivets.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I just have a question. The people who killed the Jewish people during the Programme-- were they Romanian?

speaker: interviewee
text: Romanian.

speaker: interviewer
text: Romanians.

speaker: interviewee
text: Romanians. The German didn't, at that time, as far as I know in Vyshnivets, they weren't involved. They-- they were strictly there for military purposes. But the Romanians, those that came back, some that had been in Vyshnivets before came back, and they took their vengeance out on the-- on the Yiddish population of Vyshnivets. Of course, the pain was pretty great in our family, having lost my grandfather. Even to this day, it's very painful. We've lived with that pain all our lives. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But the worst was yet to come. We didn't know what was going to happen, how to organize our lives. Parents, of course, were concerned for the safety of their children. And the whole community was very disturbed by what was going on, and very concerned about what was going to happen.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to the Jewish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Jewish school, of course, when the Soviet Union retreated, the Jewish school ceased to be a school. I, personally was, while I was attending the Jewish school, they selected-- I don't remember-- three or five top students. I was amongst one of the top students that was to be sent to the Crimea as a prize for being a top student. And my papers were ready to go. But as it happened, the war started. And that was the reason why I didn't go.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was the Russians were sending you to Crimea?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, the Russians. We-- the-- as I explained before, the paradox in Russia was great. Because on the one hand, people-- people were starving, and people were scared, and people were ending up in jails, and people were being sent to Siberia. And on the other hand, they promoted theater, they promoted learning, they encouraged studies, they gave opportunities for people, especially for those that had the brains and the will to learn. But we now know why. Because what they did, they taught you, they educated you, they perfected you, but then they used you for the rest of your lives for furthering their cause. But that was another chapter. What was going on now was that we started to feel what it was like to live under the Fascist government. We younger-- we were young boys, and men were taken, and were made to sweep the streets in town. I remember we were taken, young boys. I was only about 12, 13 years old-- 12 years old-- we were taken to work, just simply carrying rocks from one pile to another pile.

speaker: interviewer
text: For what purpose?

speaker: interviewee
text: Just to make us work. Whether that was the arrangement between the Romanians and the Germans, that they have to mistreat the Jewish people, and that was their way of mistreating them in order to appease the German philosophies, if you will, I don't know. But the Romanians had their own philosophies, which weren't much different than the German philosophies. Jewish people were to be used and destroyed. Of course, we didn't realize then what their plans were for us. And life like that under those circumstances, very, very difficult, went on, until the fall of 1941.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you still have your stores then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. The Jewish people who were deprived of any privileges of owning shops, or doing anything.

speaker: interviewer
text: How-- how did they support themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: With very great difficulty. Those that had some friends among the Ukrainian people bartered and bought wherever they could in exchange for whatever valuables they had. And a little bit of trade went on, but it was under very difficult circumstances. But we had to do what we had to do in order to survive. And we-- we survived. We survived.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there-- was there a curfew?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Yes, there was. You weren't allowed after certain hours to be on the streets.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to wear a yellow star?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not at that time. We, at that time, we really didn't know yet what-- what was in store for us. I don't remember having to wear a star yet in Vyshnivets. But it would be soon enough. We soon enough found out that-- what the plans for us were, when one day they announced that all Jewish people from Vyshnivets-- because the Jewish people from the surrounding areas were sent off to Yedinitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is Yedinitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was earlier. Yedinitz. I think, was anywhere on the outskirts of Bessarabia. I'm just not sure. It was on the border between Bucovina and Bessarabia. And I'm glad that I mentioned Yedinitz, because that's where my grandfather on my father's side, [NON-ENGLISH], and his wife [? Vicki, ?] and my aunt [? Henja, ?] the youngest one in the family, youngest sister of my dad, they were taken away with all the other Jewish people that lived in the surrounding areas of Vyshnivets in the smaller villages and the smaller towns. Marched to Yedinitz. We didn't know-- I remember saying goodbye to my grandparents. I remember just like yesterday. They didn't know where they were going. We didn't know where they were going. We didn't know what their fate would be. And here, we say goodbye. Say goodbye. Lived a lifetime together, and all of a sudden, you say goodbye, and they're gone. All we heard after they left was that apparently, they had been all gathered in the area, and they were made to suffer something about water not being available for them. That there was only one well to supply water to all these people, and the Romanians soldiers in charge would not give them any water unless they gave their valuables. We heard about that. But of course, we didn't know whether that was rumors, or whether that was true. Later, of course, we found out, unfortunately, that not only was that true, but it was a lot worse than what we were told. So that's how I lost my grandparents, and my youngest aunt. They disappeared. They were gone. Our fate was not to be any different, as we soon found out, because like I said, on a given day, they told us all the Jewish people from Vyshnivets to gather up food to take along for 24 hours, and whatever belongings they could carry with them, and the rest was to stay behind.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did they communicate this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was on a placard and pasted on the walls. And it was there for everybody to see. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Again, we didn't know where we were going. They told us that together at the station that we were going to go to another place, where there was going to be maybe work, or there was going to be-- they promised certain things.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who told you that? Who promised?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the authorities kind of let it out, in a-- in a way, that the population should-- they deceived us, like they deceived everybody. But at that time, we tended to believe in something better. We hoped. We hoped. You never gave up hope. [WIPES NOSE] And so, on a nice fall day in October, I believe it was, 1941, the parade started. The Jewish people, with their [NON-ENGLISH], with their belongings, started marching towards the station. It was about three mile, three kilometer walk.

speaker: interviewer
text: The train station?

speaker: interviewee
text: The train station. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think I remember the Vyshnivets [NON-ENGLISH], his name was [NON-ENGLISH], ended up being a rabbi in Manchester after the war. I remember him, because he was a very well-known personality in Vyshnivets. The rabbi [NON-ENGLISH], of course, left before the Soviet Union, before the Soviet Union took over. He was given information, which they told him to get out. So he left, but [NON-ENGLISH] stayed on, carried on his duties. So he marched with his family. He had a large family. And other prominent Jewish people marched with him. And everybody was walking towards the station. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Under the railroad tracks at the station, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] lot of cars, freight cars-- a cross between freight cars and cattle cars. I didn't know at that time what they were. But there were cars, empty cars. Lots of them. I don't know how many. Hundreds. And [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the people of Vyshnivets [? Juden ?] started to assemble in great numbers. I remember that I wanted to go back. I wanted to see what Vyshnivets was like without [PAUSES FOR 9 SECONDS] without its [? Juden. ?] And I was a young kid at that time. But I now know and understand what I felt, because it was a ghost town. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] They tore the Jewish heart out of it. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was nothing. Anyway, I returned to the station, and joined my family. And they told us to get into the cars. Crowded us into the cars. I don't remember how many-- 40-50 in a car. With our [NON-ENGLISH], our belongings. Belongings. We sat on the belongings; we were almost up to the top. There was a little window for air. And so they packed us all off, and the train started. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The train started. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After traveling the rest of that day and the night, and some part of the next day, I remember the train stopped. There was a bridge which the train couldn't cross with the freight, with the people in it. So they made us march across that bridge and carry our belongings on the other side.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I don't remember the-- I remember it was a river. I remember it was a narrow bridge with the rail tracks on it. And we had to walk on these planks between the-- and it was very dangerous, because we could fall into that river at any given moment. But we made it. Made it across on the other side. I also remember that we had the first two or three casualties during the night. Some older people died, and they had to be buried.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: They died in the train. They were buried on the other side, or on that side of the river. And we were given about half an hour, an hour, and we were back in the-- back in the cattle cars. And we started again.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the-- the train eventually made it across the bridge?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, yeah, without-- the train couldn't carry the freight across, because the bridge wasn't very secure. I don't know what the reason was. But that's what-- that's what happened as far as I remember. And so we started our journey to the unknown destination. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [SIGHS] And for the first time in my life, even though I was only 13, 14 years old, I started to feel what it was like without a home.

speaker: interviewee
text: What are we doing in the middle of nowhere? Where are we going? But you know, as bad as it is, you find strength of being with your family. You cling together. And so we continued on our journey, and I don't remember really whether it was two days and two nights, or one day and two nights. It really doesn't matter.

speaker: interviewee
text: Our final stop came in the early hours on the third day, I believe, of having been in that car. We were not allowed out of the car after just crossing that bridge. We were in the car all that time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people do for toilet in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't remember. I honestly don't remember.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did people eat in the train?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had whatever provisions we took along. We still had a little bit of food, and we had some belongings. I do remember, though, that everybody left their homes. My mother cleaned the house. She left everything intact. We had a-- a cow, a milk cow. And my dad took care that the cow was properly left with food and water. But anyway, we were, as I said, on the train, until the train finally came to a stop on the third day. It was still dark outside, and it was in the early hours. And after about an hour of the train sitting in one spot, the doors opened. Doors opened, and the shouting [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] out of the cars, I don't remember whether it was Romanian or [NON-ENGLISH], or whatever it was. Out of the cars. And so we were only glad to get out of the cars. But it was dark. We didn't know where we were. We didn't know nothing. But we were glad to get out. So I remember jumping out of the car and landing in a-- in a ditch full of mud and water. I also remember that no sooner I jumped out, I heard the sound of a very forceful river. Didn't know where we were. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] But after everybody got out, and we organized ourselves with our belongings, they pointed us to march in a certain direction. So the people started marching in a direction, and following each other. We were surrounded by Romanian Gendarmes with their bayonets. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There may have even been some dogs, but we didn't see very much. It was dark. But later, when the-- when morning started to break, we saw everybody.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you know the name of that place? We later-- we didn't know at that time. But we later found out that we were close to the Dniester River bordering Bessarabia and the Ukraine. And this town that we were marched to was called Ataki.

speaker: interviewee
text: But as we were marching in a certain direction, we started to see in the distance lights. Lights. Little lights. We didn't know what those lights were, but we were walking in the direction of those lights. And I believe it took about an hour to get to the area. And as we got closer, the lights became more clearer. But we still didn't know what kind of lights they were. Finally, we ended up in the middle of a large field crowded with hundreds and hundreds of people, and everybody-- or most everybody-- had a little lantern in front of him. And those were those lights that we saw. Those people had been there apparently for longer for a day or two, and they brought with them [? premises ?] and lights--

speaker: interviewer
text: From home?

speaker: interviewee
text: From home. And we realized that they had maybe been a more affluent-- from a more affluent area, or more-- better prepared than we were. But anyway, we soon started to join those people, and find a little spot where we could lay down our belongings. And just being so tired, we wanted to sit down. And we wanted to-- and conversations were struck up between those people and our people.

speaker: interviewer
text: So who-- who were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were Jewish people from Suceava, and from other areas. But mostly Suceava. Maybe [NON-ENGLISH], too. I am not sure. I-- I. Anyway, I was very tired. I was very tired. And I remember as soon as we put some of our belongings down, I fell on my belongings, and I fell asleep. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And didn't know what was happening until I woke up when day started to break. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And the first thing I heard was a conversation between my dad and some other man that made a reference to the fact that they had to bury some people. As it turned out, the people sitting not far from where we were was a family of three of a husband and wife, and a mother, and the husband was a druggist. And I was given to understand from the conversation that apparently, they decided to commit suicide.

speaker: interviewer
text: All three?

speaker: interviewee
text: The husband asked the wife whether he wants her to join him. And she said no. And he asked his mother. And she said yes. So the man and his mother committed suicide. And of course, they died immediately. And my dad felt that they should be buried before we continue on with our march.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did-- did you know that you were going to march somewhere else?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We kind of hoped, because we were in the middle of nowhere. And soon enough, shouts, shots in the air, screaming, beating. And they were chasing us in a certain direction. And everybody started marching in that direction. We didn't know where we were going.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. This is a good place to stop. Thank you.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
